title: Python for Beginners: Write Working Programs from Day One
tagline: Learn to write Python programs that solve real problems, automate tasks, and launch your programming journey with confidence.
target_audience: Complete programming beginners with no prior coding experience, career changers looking to enter tech fields, students preparing for computer science studies, professionals seeking to automate tasks in their current roles, and hobbyists interested in building personal projects. Assumes basic computer literacy but no technical background.
prerequisites: Basic computer skills: creating/saving files, navigating folders, using a text editor
Ability to install software on their computer (Python interpreter and a code editor)
Comfort with basic arithmetic and logical thinking
learning_outcomes: Write and execute Python programs using variables, data types, and operators to process information and produce output
Build programs that make decisions using conditional statements and automate repetitive tasks with for and while loops
Define reusable functions with parameters and return values to organize code and solve modular problems
Store and manipulate structured data using lists and dictionaries to model real-world information
Process text files by reading data, transforming it with string methods, and writing results to disk
Debug programs systematically by interpreting error messages and applying troubleshooting techniques
Import and use standard library modules to leverage Python's built-in capabilities without reinventing solutions
Build a complete multi-feature application that integrates variables, control flow, functions, data structures, and file I/O

modules.title: Getting Started: Your First Python Program
modules.description: Install Python, run code in the interactive shell, and write your first script that produces visible output.
learning_objectives: Install Python and a code editor on your computer
Execute Python code in the REPL (interactive shell) and run saved script files
Write programs using print statements, basic arithmetic, and comments
Identify and fix simple syntax errors by reading error messages

lessons.title: Installing Python and Setting Up Your Environment
key_teaching_points: Download and install Python from python.org, verifying installation in terminal/command prompt
Choose and install a beginner-friendly code editor (Thonny, VS Code, or IDLE)
Understand the difference between the Python interpreter and a code editor
Navigate to the correct directory and run 'python --version' to confirm setup

lessons.title: The Python REPL: Your Interactive Playground
key_teaching_points: Launch the Python REPL and use it as an interactive calculator
Understand immediate feedback: type expression, see result instantly
Recognize when you're in the REPL (>>> prompt) vs. terminal
Practice basic arithmetic operations (+, -, *, /, //, %, **) with live results

lessons.title: Writing and Running Your First Script
key_teaching_points: Create a .py file, write print('Hello, World!'), and save it
Run the script from terminal using 'python filename.py'
Understand the difference between REPL (interactive) and script (saved file) execution
Add comments using # to document what your code does
Intentionally create and then fix a syntax error to practice reading error messages

modules.hands_on_activity: Build a 'Personal Introduction' program that prints your name, hometown, and three favorite hobbies using multiple print statements. Run it from the terminal and modify it to include calculated information (like your age in days by multiplying years by 365).
key_takeaways: The REPL provides instant feedback for trying Python expressions; scripts save code for repeated execution
Python files end in .py and run from the terminal using 'python filename.py'
Error messages point to the line and type of problem—they're helpful guides, not failures
Comments (starting with #) explain code to humans and are ignored by Python

modules.title: Variables and Data Types: Storing Information
modules.description: Store values in named variables and work with Python's core data types: integers, floats, strings, and booleans.
learning_objectives: Create variables with meaningful names and assign values using the = operator
Distinguish between integers, floats, strings, and booleans and when to use each
Perform operations appropriate to each data type (arithmetic, concatenation, comparison)
Convert between data types using int(), float(), str(), and understand when conversion is necessary

lessons.title: Variables: Naming and Storing Values
key_teaching_points: Variables are labels that reference values stored in memory, like labeled boxes
Assignment uses = (not the same as mathematical equality or ==)
Variable naming rules: start with letter/underscore, use snake_case, make names descriptive
Variables can be reassigned to new values, changing what the name references
Python is dynamically typed—you don't declare types, but every value has one

lessons.title: Numbers: Integers and Floats
key_teaching_points: Integers are whole numbers (42, -17, 0); floats have decimal points (3.14, -0.5)
Arithmetic operators: +, -, *, / (always returns float), // (floor division), % (remainder), ** (exponent)
Order of operations (PEMDAS) applies; use parentheses to control evaluation order
Common mistake: / vs. // for division—understand when you want decimals vs. whole numbers

lessons.title: Strings: Working with Text
key_teaching_points: Strings are sequences of characters in quotes (single or double, be consistent)
Concatenation with + joins strings; multiplication with * repeats them
Strings are immutable—operations create new strings, don't modify originals
Use len() to get string length; introduce basic indexing with [0] for first character

lessons.title: Booleans, Type Conversion, and Input
key_teaching_points: Booleans are True or False (capitalized); result from comparisons (==, !=, <, >, <=, >=)
input() gets user input as a string—always returns string even if user types number
Convert types explicitly: int('42'), float('3.14'), str(100)
Common error: forgetting to convert input() result before arithmetic

modules.hands_on_activity: Create a 'Personal Calculator' that prompts the user for two numbers using input(), converts them to floats, performs all arithmetic operations (+, -, *, /, //, %, **), and displays the results with descriptive labels. Test with whole numbers and decimals to see the difference between / and //.
key_takeaways: Variables store references to values; assignment with = creates or updates these references
Python has four core types: int (whole numbers), float (decimals), str (text), bool (True/False)
input() always returns a string—use int() or float() to convert before math operations
= assigns values; == tests equality; don't confuse them in your code

modules.title: Making Decisions with Conditional Statements
modules.description: Write programs that make decisions and execute different code paths based on conditions using if, elif, and else.
learning_objectives: Write if statements that execute code only when a condition is True
Chain multiple conditions using elif to test alternatives in order
Provide fallback behavior with else when no conditions match
Combine conditions using logical operators (and, or, not) to express complex logic

lessons.title: The if Statement: Conditional Execution
key_teaching_points: if tests a condition; the indented code block runs only if the condition is True
Indentation defines the code block—Python uses whitespace, not curly braces
Comparison operators create boolean conditions: ==, !=, <, >, <=, >=
Common mistake: using = instead of == in conditions assigns rather than compares

lessons.title: elif and else: Multiple Branches
key_teaching_points: elif (else if) chains alternatives—tested in order, first True condition wins
else catches everything that didn't match previous conditions—no condition needed
Only ONE branch executes in an if/elif/else chain, then Python moves on
Order matters: put specific conditions before general ones or they'll never run

lessons.title: Logical Operators and Complex Conditions
key_teaching_points: and requires both conditions True; or requires at least one True; not inverts True/False
Use parentheses to group complex conditions and control evaluation order
Short-circuit evaluation: 'and' stops at first False; 'or' stops at first True
in operator tests membership: 'a' in 'apple' is True

modules.hands_on_activity: Build a 'Grade Calculator' that asks for a numerical score (0-100), then uses if/elif/else to display the letter grade (A: 90-100, B: 80-89, C: 70-79, D: 60-69, F: below 60) with appropriate messages. Add validation that checks if the score is outside 0-100 and displays an error message.
key_takeaways: Indentation is syntax in Python—it defines which code belongs to which block
if/elif/else chains test conditions in order; the first True condition executes, then the chain exits
Use == for comparison, = for assignment; mixing them is a common beginner error
Logical operators (and, or, not) combine simple conditions into complex decision logic

modules.title: Automating Repetition with Loops
modules.description: Use for and while loops to automate repetitive tasks, iterate over sequences, and process collections of data.
learning_objectives: Write for loops that iterate over ranges and sequences to repeat actions
Use while loops to repeat code until a condition becomes False
Accumulate results using variables that update inside loops
Control loop execution with break (exit early) and continue (skip to next iteration)

lessons.title: for Loops: Iterating Over Sequences
key_teaching_points: for loops iterate over sequences—each item gets assigned to the loop variable
range(n) generates numbers from 0 to n-1; range(start, stop, step) for more control
Common mistake: range(10) gives 0-9, not 1-10; off-by-one errors are frequent
Loop variables persist after the loop ends with the last value

lessons.title: while Loops: Repeating Until a Condition Changes
key_teaching_points: while loops continue as long as the condition remains True—check condition before each iteration
Must update variables inside the loop that affect the condition, or loop runs forever
Infinite loops are bugs: press Ctrl+C to stop runaway programs
while is useful when you don't know how many iterations you need in advance

lessons.title: Loop Patterns: Accumulation and Control
key_teaching_points: Accumulator pattern: initialize variable before loop, update it inside loop each iteration
break exits the loop immediately; continue skips remaining code and starts next iteration
Nested loops: inner loop completes fully for each iteration of outer loop
Use meaningful loop variable names (for student in students, not just for i in x)

modules.hands_on_activity: Create a 'Number Guessing Game' where the program picks a random number (1-100) using import random and random.randint(). Use a while loop to let the user guess repeatedly. After each guess, tell them if they're too high or too low. When they guess correctly, display how many attempts it took and break out of the loop.
key_takeaways: for loops iterate over known sequences; while loops continue until a condition changes
range(n) produces 0 through n-1, not 1 through n—watch for off-by-one errors
Accumulator pattern (initialize before loop, update inside) builds up results across iterations
break exits the loop entirely; continue skips to the next iteration

modules.title: Organizing Code with Functions
modules.description: Define reusable functions that take inputs, perform operations, and return results to eliminate repetition and organize code.
learning_objectives: Define functions using def with parameters and return values
Call functions by name with arguments and capture returned values
Explain the difference between defining a function (creating it) and calling it (using it)
Use functions to eliminate code duplication and break problems into smaller pieces

lessons.title: Defining and Calling Functions
key_teaching_points: def creates a function; indented code is the function body that runs when called
Parameters are placeholders in the function definition; arguments are actual values passed when calling
return sends a value back to the caller; function without return gives back None
Critical distinction: defining a function (def) doesn't run it; calling it (function_name()) does

lessons.title: Parameters, Arguments, and Return Values
key_teaching_points: Functions can have multiple parameters separated by commas
Arguments are passed in order (positional) or by name (keyword arguments)
return immediately exits the function and sends the value back
Common error: confusing print() with return—print displays, return sends values to caller

lessons.title: Scope and Function Design
key_teaching_points: Variables defined inside functions are local—they don't exist outside the function
Functions can access global variables but shouldn't modify them (use parameters and return instead)
Good functions do one clear task, have descriptive names, and return results rather than printing
DRY principle: Don't Repeat Yourself—if you copy-paste code, make it a function

modules.hands_on_activity: Build a 'Temperature Converter' with three functions: celsius_to_fahrenheit(), fahrenheit_to_celsius(), and a main menu function that asks the user which conversion they want, gets the temperature, calls the appropriate function, and displays the result. Test all functions with known values (0°C = 32°F, 100°C = 212°F).
key_takeaways: def defines a function (the recipe); calling it by name runs the code (follows the recipe)
return sends values back to caller; print() displays to screen—they serve different purposes
Parameters are placeholders in the definition; arguments are actual values when calling
Functions eliminate repetition, make code testable, and break complex problems into manageable pieces

modules.title: Working with Lists: Ordered Collections
modules.description: Create and manipulate lists to store sequences of items, iterate over them, and transform collections of data.
learning_objectives: Create lists with square brackets and access items by index (including negative indices)
Add items with append() and extend(), remove items with remove() and pop()
Iterate over lists with for loops and build new lists from existing ones
Slice lists to extract portions and understand list mutability

lessons.title: Creating Lists and Accessing Items
key_teaching_points: Lists store ordered sequences in square brackets: [item1, item2, item3]
Access items by zero-based index: first_item = my_list[0]
Negative indices count from end: my_list[-1] is last item, [-2] is second-to-last
IndexError occurs when accessing an index that doesn't exist
len(my_list) returns number of items; use len() - 1 for last valid index

lessons.title: Modifying Lists: Adding and Removing Items
key_teaching_points: Lists are mutable—they can change after creation unlike strings
append(item) adds to end; insert(index, item) adds at specific position
remove(value) deletes first matching item; pop(index) removes and returns item at index
Methods like append() and sort() modify the list in-place and return None (not a new list)

lessons.title: Iterating and Slicing Lists
key_teaching_points: for item in my_list iterates over each element directly
for i in range(len(my_list)) iterates by index when you need positions
Slicing extracts portions: my_list[1:4] gets items at indices 1, 2, 3 (not 4)
Slice notation [start:stop:step]; omit values for defaults (start=0, stop=end, step=1)
List comprehensions provide concise way to transform lists: [x*2 for x in numbers]

modules.hands_on_activity: Create a 'To-Do List Manager' that starts with an empty list. Build a menu that lets users: (1) add a task, (2) view all tasks with numbers, (3) mark a task complete by removing it by index, (4) quit. Use a while loop for the menu and demonstrate append(), list iteration, and pop().
key_takeaways: Lists are mutable ordered sequences accessed by zero-based index; negative indices count from the end
append() adds to end; remove() deletes by value; pop() removes by index and returns the item
Slicing [start:stop] extracts portions; stop index is exclusive (not included in result)
List methods like append() and sort() modify in-place and return None, not a new list

modules.title: Dictionaries: Storing Key-Value Relationships
modules.description: Use dictionaries to model real-world entities and relationships by storing data as key-value pairs.
learning_objectives: Create dictionaries with curly braces and access values by key
Add, modify, and remove key-value pairs dynamically
Iterate over keys, values, and key-value pairs with dictionary methods
Choose between lists and dictionaries based on whether you need order/indexing or named access

lessons.title: Creating and Accessing Dictionaries
key_teaching_points: Dictionaries store key-value pairs in curly braces: {'name': 'Alice', 'age': 30}
Access values by key: person['name'] returns 'Alice'
KeyError occurs when accessing a key that doesn't exist; use .get(key, default) for safety
Keys must be immutable (strings, numbers, tuples); values can be any type
Dictionaries model real-world entities: person, book, student—keys are attributes

lessons.title: Modifying Dictionaries and Checking Membership
key_teaching_points: Add or update entries: dict[key] = value (creates if new, updates if exists)
Remove entries: del dict[key] or dict.pop(key) which also returns the value
in operator checks if key exists: if 'name' in person
Dictionaries are mutable like lists—they can change after creation

lessons.title: Iterating Over Dictionaries
key_teaching_points: for key in dict iterates over keys only
.values() returns all values; .keys() returns all keys; .items() returns (key, value) tuples
for key, value in dict.items() unpacks tuples for convenient access to both
Dictionary order is preserved in Python 3.7+, but dictionaries are conceptually unordered

modules.hands_on_activity: Build a 'Contact Book' that stores names as keys and phone numbers as values in a dictionary. Create functions to: add a contact, search for a contact by name (using .get() to handle missing names gracefully), display all contacts, and remove a contact. Use a menu loop to call these functions.
key_takeaways: Dictionaries map keys to values like a real dictionary maps words to definitions
Access by key with dict[key]; use .get(key, default) to avoid KeyError when key might not exist
Use .items() with for key, value in dict.items() to iterate over both keys and values
Choose dictionaries when you need named access to values; choose lists when order and numeric indexing matter

modules.title: String Manipulation and Text Processing
modules.description: Process and transform text using string methods, slicing, formatting, and common text patterns.
learning_objectives: Extract parts of strings using slicing and indexing
Transform strings with methods like .lower(), .upper(), .strip(), .replace(), and .split()
Format output using f-strings to embed variables in text
Process text data line-by-line and word-by-word for analysis

lessons.title: String Slicing and Indexing
key_teaching_points: Strings are sequences of characters; access by index just like lists: text[0]
Slicing extracts substrings: text[2:5], text[:3], text[3:] work like list slicing
Strings are immutable—slicing and methods return new strings, don't modify originals
Common use: extract file extensions, parse formatted data, get first/last characters

lessons.title: Essential String Methods
key_teaching_points: .lower() and .upper() change case; .strip() removes leading/trailing whitespace
.replace(old, new) substitutes text; returns new string (original unchanged)
.split(separator) breaks string into list of parts; .join(list) combines list into string
Method chaining: text.strip().lower().split() applies methods left-to-right

lessons.title: String Formatting with f-strings
key_teaching_points: f-strings embed variables in text: f'Hello, {name}! You are {age} years old.'
Expressions work inside {}: f'Total: {price * quantity}'
Format numbers: f'{value:.2f}' for 2 decimal places, f'{number:,}' for thousands separator
f-strings are modern Python (3.6+); older code uses .format() or % formatting

modules.hands_on_activity: Create a 'Text Analyzer' that asks the user for a sentence, then displays: total characters (with and without spaces), word count (using .split()), frequency of a user-specified word (using .lower() and .count()), and the sentence in all caps, all lowercase, and title case. Use f-strings for all output.
key_takeaways: Strings are immutable—methods return new strings rather than modifying the original
.split() breaks strings into lists of words; .join() combines lists back into strings
f-strings embed variables and expressions in text: f'Result: {variable}' is clean and readable
.strip(), .lower(), and .replace() are workhorses for cleaning and normalizing text data

modules.title: Reading and Writing Files
modules.description: Make programs persist data beyond execution by reading from and writing to text files on disk.
learning_objectives: Open files for reading and writing using open() with appropriate modes
Read entire files, read line-by-line, and write content to files
Use the with statement to ensure files close properly even if errors occur
Process file data using loops, string methods, and data structures

lessons.title: Reading Files
key_teaching_points: open('filename.txt', 'r') opens file for reading; 'r' is read mode
.read() returns entire file as single string; .readlines() returns list of lines
with open(...) as file automatically closes file when block ends—best practice
Iterate over file object directly for memory efficiency: for line in file

lessons.title: Writing Files
key_teaching_points: open('filename.txt', 'w') opens for writing; 'w' mode erases existing content
'a' mode appends to end without erasing; 'x' creates new file (fails if exists)
.write(string) writes text to file; doesn't add newlines automatically (use \n)
print(text, file=file_object) writes with automatic newline

lessons.title: Processing File Data
key_teaching_points: Read files line-by-line in a loop to process large files without loading all into memory
Use .strip() to remove \n newline characters at end of each line
Combine file operations with lists and dictionaries to analyze and transform data
Common pattern: read data, process it in memory (lists/dicts), write results to new file

modules.hands_on_activity: Build an 'Expense Tracker' that reads transactions from a text file (one per line: 'Category: Amount'). Parse each line using .split(':'), store expenses in a dictionary where keys are categories and values are lists of amounts. Calculate total spending per category and overall total, then write a summary report to a new file.
key_takeaways: with open(filename, mode) as file ensures files close properly—always use this pattern
'r' reads, 'w' writes (erasing existing content), 'a' appends; default is 'r' if mode omitted
Iterate over file object directly (for line in file) for memory-efficient line-by-line processing
Combining file I/O with data structures (lists, dictionaries) enables powerful data processing

modules.title: Error Handling and Debugging
modules.description: Write robust programs that handle errors gracefully and develop systematic debugging skills.
learning_objectives: Distinguish between syntax errors (caught before running) and runtime errors (exceptions during execution)
Use try/except blocks to catch exceptions and prevent crashes
Read and interpret error messages to identify problem location and type
Apply systematic debugging techniques including print statements and step-through analysis

lessons.title: Understanding Errors and Reading Tracebacks
key_teaching_points: Syntax errors prevent code from running; runtime errors (exceptions) occur during execution
Tracebacks read bottom-to-top: error type and message at bottom, line numbers above
Common exceptions: NameError (undefined variable), TypeError (wrong type), ValueError (right type, wrong value)
Error messages are helpful guides—read them carefully rather than panicking

lessons.title: Handling Errors with try/except
key_teaching_points: try block contains code that might fail; except block runs if an error occurs
Catch specific exceptions: except ValueError handles only ValueErrors
Bare except catches all exceptions—use sparingly, better to be specific
try/except prevents crashes, lets programs respond gracefully to invalid input or missing files

lessons.title: Debugging Strategies
key_teaching_points: Print debugging: add print statements to show variable values at key points
Rubber duck debugging: explain code line-by-line to find logic errors
Comment out code sections to isolate where problems occur
Test incrementally—write small pieces, verify they work, then add more

modules.hands_on_activity: Enhance an existing program (like the number guessing game or calculator) with comprehensive error handling. Use try/except to catch ValueError when converting user input to numbers, handle FileNotFoundError when opening files, and validate that numbers are in expected ranges. Test with intentionally bad input to verify error handling works.
key_takeaways: Tracebacks show error type, message, and line number—read from bottom to top
try/except catches runtime errors so programs can respond gracefully instead of crashing
Catch specific exceptions (ValueError, FileNotFoundError) rather than using bare except
Print debugging is simple but effective—display variable values to see what's actually happening

modules.title: Modules and the Standard Library
modules.description: Leverage Python's standard library by importing modules and learn to explore available functionality.
learning_objectives: Import modules using import and use their functions with dot notation
Use key standard library modules: random, datetime, math, os
Read module documentation to discover available functions and their usage
Understand when to import specific functions with from module import function

lessons.title: Importing and Using Modules
key_teaching_points: Modules are Python files containing functions and variables you can import
import module_name brings in the module; use functions with module.function()
from module import function imports specific functions to use without module prefix
import module as alias creates shorter names: import datetime as dt

lessons.title: Essential Standard Library Modules
key_teaching_points: random: generate random numbers, choose random items, shuffle lists
datetime: work with dates and times, calculate durations, format timestamps
math: mathematical functions (sqrt, floor, ceil, pi, trigonometry)
os and pathlib: interact with file system, check if files exist, create directories
Standard library is built-in—no installation needed, just import and use

lessons.title: Exploring Documentation and help()
key_teaching_points: help(module) or help(function) shows documentation in the REPL
dir(module) lists all available functions and attributes
Official Python docs at docs.python.org document all standard library modules
Learning to read docs builds independence—you don't need to memorize everything

modules.hands_on_activity: Build a 'Password Generator' that uses the random module to create secure passwords. Let users specify length and whether to include uppercase, lowercase, numbers, and symbols. Use random.choice() to select characters from appropriate strings. Add a feature using datetime to timestamp when passwords were generated and save the log to a file.
key_takeaways: import module loads a module; use functions with module.function() syntax
Python's standard library provides tools for common tasks—don't reinvent the wheel
help() and dir() let you explore modules interactively; docs.python.org has comprehensive reference
from module import function imports specific items for use without the module prefix

modules.title: Capstone Project: Building a Complete Application
modules.description: Integrate everything you've learned to build a multi-feature program that solves a real-world problem.
learning_objectives: Design a program structure that uses functions to organize different features
Combine variables, control flow, loops, data structures, and file I/O in a single application
Implement error handling to make the program robust against invalid input
Test the program systematically and debug issues that arise

lessons.title: Planning Your Application
key_teaching_points: Break complex programs into features, then break features into functions
Sketch the main menu and user flow before writing code
Identify what data structures you need: lists for sequences, dictionaries for entities
Plan file format: how will you store and retrieve data persistently?

lessons.title: Building Core Features
key_teaching_points: Start with one feature, get it working completely, then add the next
Write functions with clear purposes—each does one thing well
Test each function individually before integrating into the main program
Use meaningful variable names so code reads like English

lessons.title: Adding Persistence and Polish
key_teaching_points: Save program state to a file so data persists between runs
Add input validation and error handling with try/except
Provide clear prompts and formatted output so users understand the interface
Test edge cases: empty data, invalid input, missing files

lessons.title: Testing, Debugging, and Refinement
key_teaching_points: Run through every menu option and feature systematically
Try to break your program with bad input—then fix the crashes
Add helpful error messages and validation
Consider user experience: Is it clear what to do? Is output readable?

modules.hands_on_activity: Build a complete 'Personal Library Manager' that stores books (title, author, year, read status) in a list of dictionaries and saves to a JSON file. Features: add book, view all books, search by title or author, mark book as read, delete book, show statistics (total books, books read, average publication year). Use functions for each feature, a while loop for the menu, error handling for file operations and user input, and provide formatted output.
key_takeaways: Complex programs are built incrementally—one working feature at a time, tested before moving on
Functions organize code by purpose; each function should do one clear thing
File I/O makes programs practical—data persists and programs become tools, not toys
Planning before coding (what features? what data structures? what file format?) prevents getting stuck

quiz_topics: Identifying correct variable naming conventions and recognizing invalid names
Distinguishing between assignment (=) and comparison (==) operators in code
Predicting output of code snippets using if/elif/else conditional logic
Determining the output of for loops with range() and off-by-one scenarios
Identifying when to use while vs. for loops based on problem requirements
Analyzing functions to determine what values are returned vs. printed
Selecting correct list methods for adding, removing, and modifying items
Choosing between lists and dictionaries based on data access patterns
Identifying correct file modes ('r', 'w', 'a') for different file operations
Reading error messages to identify exception types and affected line numbers
assessment.capstone_project: Build a 'Personal Finance Tracker' application that helps users manage their budget. The program stores income and expense transactions (date, category, amount, description) in a list of dictionaries and persists data to a JSON file. Core features include: add transaction (with input validation), view all transactions sorted by date, calculate total income vs. expenses and show balance, display spending by category using a dictionary to aggregate amounts, search transactions by category or date range, and generate a summary report saved to a text file. The application must use functions to organize each feature, implement error handling for file operations and invalid input, provide a clear menu-driven interface with a while loop, and demonstrate proficiency with variables, conditionals, loops, functions, lists, dictionaries, string formatting, and file I/O. Students deliver working code and a brief text file explaining their design choices.
grading_criteria: Functionality: Program runs without crashes and all required features work correctly with valid input (40%)
Code organization: Effective use of functions to separate concerns; each function has a clear single purpose (20%)
Data structures: Appropriate use of lists and dictionaries to model and manipulate transaction data (15%)
Error handling: Try/except blocks prevent crashes from invalid input and file errors; validation provides helpful messages (10%)
File persistence: Data correctly saves to and loads from file; program state persists between sessions (10%)
Code quality: Meaningful variable names, appropriate comments, consistent style, and readable formatting (5%)

recommended_tools: Python 3.8 or newer (download from python.org) - the programming language itself
Thonny IDE - beginner-friendly editor with built-in debugger and variable inspector
VS Code with Python extension - professional editor with excellent Python support (alternative to Thonny)
Python Tutor (pythontutor.com) - web-based visualizer for understanding code execution step-by-step
A text editor (Notepad++, Sublime Text, or built-in system editor) - for viewing and editing .py files
Terminal/Command Prompt - for running Python scripts from the command line
Official Python documentation (docs.python.org) - comprehensive reference for syntax and standard library
next_steps: Intermediate Python course covering list comprehensions, decorators, generators, object-oriented programming, and advanced data structures
Web development with Flask or Django to build web applications with Python
Data analysis with pandas and NumPy to process and visualize datasets
Automation scripting: use Python to automate file management, web scraping, and system administration tasks
Problem-solving practice on HackerRank, LeetCode (Easy problems), or Exercism Python track to solidify fundamentals
Automate the Boring Stuff with Python by Al Sweigart - free book focused on practical automation projects
Build personal projects: task manager, weather app using APIs, simple games, or tools for your hobbies/work
Join Python communities: r/learnpython subreddit, Python Discord servers, local meetups or study groups
